topics.first_post_excerpt:
Summary: Important long-term features see great progress with the new release preCICE release v3.2, such as different options to handle dynamic meshes. Improved memory footprint of mesh data structures

How did preCICE get popular?

In 2016, the German Research Foundation (DFG) published a call for proposals on Research Software Sustainability. Its goal was to build infrastructures and develop methods to make research software more sustainable and available for larger audiences. topics.title: Troubleshooting: Unable to locate a modulefile for '<package>'
topics.fancy_title: Troubleshooting: Unable to locate a modulefile for ‘<package>’
topics.first_post_excerpt: This error might occur after you attempt to load packages for the first time after installing a module manager. Often times this is simply due to Spack holding an outdated module path and can be fixed by again sourcing the spack/share/spack/setup-env.sh file. Alternatively reloading the .bashrc file (or the equivalent of your shell) should also work.

topics.title: Troubleshooting: Error: Conflicts in concretized spec
topics.fancy_title: Troubleshooting: Error: Conflicts in concretized spec
topics.first_post_excerpt:
There might be conflicts between dependencies. If conflicts are detected, Spack prints a large error message starting with Error: Conflicts in concretized spec when attempting to execute spack install or spack spec. This can likely be remedied by removing optional extensions of dependency packages.
Example:

topics.fancy_title: Troubleshooting: preCICE from Spack freezing

If you build preCICE with a different version/implementation of MPI than you used for your solver, you may encounter unexpected behavior early at runtime, e.g. during configuration. In that case, make sure to build preCICE with the same version, e.g.:
spack install precice ^openmpi@1.10.2

You could also tell Spack to use the MPI library installed in your system.

topics.title: Troubleshooting: preCICE from Spack and Boost-related errors
topics.fancy_title: Troubleshooting: preCICE from Spack and Boost-related errors
topics.first_post_excerpt:
By default, Spack will use the latest version of Boost. As Boost changes frequently, it is possible to get an error either because of a change that we have not yet incorporated, or because your compiler is too old. In that case, please open an issue.
Typical example (GCC 5.4.0, Boost 1.70.0, preCICE 1.5.0):
error: 'stream_socket_service' in namespace 'boost::asio' does not name a template type

In

topics.title: Troubleshooting: Mixing up dependencies from system and Spack
topics.fancy_title: Troubleshooting: Mixing up dependencies from system and Spack
topics.first_post_excerpt:
In case you see that Spack reports an error in which points to a non-spack directory, maybe you are forcing your compiler to look at places it shouldn’t. Please avoid setting environment variables such as CPLUS_INCLUDE_PATH, LD_LIBRARY_PATH, or LIBRARY_PATH when using Spack. This can particularly lead to problems if you have a (broken) installation of a requested dependency in that path.
Keep in m

topics.title: Coupled Phase-Field Brittle Fracture Simulation
topics.fancy_title: Coupled Phase-Field Brittle Fracture Simulation
topics.first_post_excerpt:
A phase-field brittle fracture mechanics code written in Nutils is coupled to a Poisson equation in FEniCS or DOLFIN. The FEniCS code is a place-holder for a proper corrosion model. The purpose of this code is to showcase how preCICE could be used to volume-couple between an electro-chemistry model (here the FEniCS code) and a fracture mechanics model. Currently, meaningless data is exchanged.
The

topics.title: What dynamic mesh solver could better avoid mesh distortion when using displacementLaplacian solver?
topics.fancy_title: What dynamic mesh solver could better avoid mesh distortion when using displacementLaplacian solver?
topics.first_post_excerpt:
Hi everyone,

topics.title: Welcome to the preCICE Forum on Discourse
topics.fancy_title: Welcome to the preCICE Forum on Discourse
topics.first_post_excerpt:
What is preCICE?
preCICE is a coupling library for partitioned multi-physics simulations, such as fluid-structure interaction or conjugate heat transfer simulations. preCICE couples existing programs capable of simulating a subpart of the complete physics involved in a simulation. This forum is about discussing present and future use-cases of the library as well as helping each other to achieve i
